([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): 東京駅, [Hepburn](/wiki/Hepburn_romanization "Hepburn romanization"): *Tōkyō-eki*; pronounced [[to̞ːkʲo̞ːe̞kʲi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese")) is a major railway station and a central rail hub in [Chiyoda, Tokyo](/wiki/Chiyoda,_Tokyo "Chiyoda, Tokyo"), Japan. The original station is located in Chiyoda's [Marunouchi](/wiki/Marunouchi "Marunouchi") business district near the [Imperial Palace](/wiki/Tokyo_Imperial_Palace "Tokyo Imperial Palace") grounds. The newer Eastern extension is not far from the [Ginza](/wiki/Ginza "Ginza") commercial district. Due to the large area covered by the station, it is divided into the Marunouchi (west) and Yaesu (east) sides in its directional signage.

**Tokyo Station** ([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): 東京駅, [Hepburn](/wiki/Hepburn_romanization "Hepburn romanization"): *Tōkyō-eki*; pronounced [[to̞ːkʲo̞ːe̞kʲi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese")) is a major railway station and a central rail hub in [Chiyoda, Tokyo](/wiki/Chiyoda,_Tokyo "Chiyoda, Tokyo"), Japan. The original station is located in Chiyoda's [Marunouchi](/wiki/Marunouchi "Marunouchi") business district near the [Imperial Palace](/wiki/Tokyo_Imperial_Palace "Tokyo Imperial Palace") grounds. The newer Eastern extension is not far from the [Ginza](/wiki/Ginza "Ginza") commercial district. Due to the large area covered by the station, it is divided into the Marunouchi (west) and Yaesu (east) sides in its directional signage.

The station opened in 1914 as an integrated terminus for the present-day [Tōkaidō Line](/wiki/T%C5%8Dkaid%C5%8D_Main_Line "Tōkaidō Main Line"), [Tōhoku Line](/wiki/T%C5%8Dhoku_Main_Line "Tōhoku Main Line"), and later the [Chūō Line](/wiki/Ch%C5%AB%C5%8D_Main_Line "Chūō Main Line"), which previously had separate termini in Tokyo. Since then, it has served as the main terminus for inter-city trains departing Tokyo westwards. The station was badly damaged during the [Bombing of Tokyo](/wiki/Bombing_of_Tokyo "Bombing of Tokyo") on 25 May 1945 but soon resumed service. The [Tōkaidō Shinkansen](/wiki/Tokaido_Shinkansen "Tokaido Shinkansen"), the world’s first dedicated high-speed rail system, opened between the station and Osaka in 1964. With the extension of northbound Shinkansen lines from [Ueno](/wiki/Ueno_Station "Ueno Station") in 1991, the station also became a gateway to northeast Japan.

Served by the [high-speed rail](/wiki/High-speed_rail "High-speed rail") lines of the [Shinkansen](/wiki/Shinkansen "Shinkansen") network, Tokyo Station is the main [inter-city rail](/wiki/Inter-city_rail "Inter-city rail") terminal in Tokyo. It is the busiest station in Japan in terms of scheduled trains, with more than 4,000 trains arriving and departing daily.[[1]](#cite_note-JT2014-1) and also ranks [sixth busiest in Japan and ninth in the world in terms of passenger throughput](/wiki/List_of_busiest_railway_stations "List of busiest railway stations");[[2]](#cite_note-2) on average, more than 500,000 people use Tokyo Station every day.[[1]](#cite_note-JT2014-1) The station is also served by many regional commuter lines of [Japan Railways](/wiki/Japan_Railways "Japan Railways"), as well as the [Tokyo Metro](/wiki/Tokyo_Metro "Tokyo Metro") network.

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Station&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

Tokyo Station serves as the [interchange station](/wiki/Interchange_station "Interchange station") for both conventional and [Shinkansen](/wiki/Shinkansen "Shinkansen") lines. The station is served by many railway lines operated by [JR East](/wiki/JR_East "JR East"). For conventional lines, the station is the terminus of the [Tōkaidō Main Line](/wiki/T%C5%8Dkaid%C5%8D_Main_Line "Tōkaidō Main Line"), the [Chūō Main Line](/wiki/Ch%C5%AB%C5%8D_Main_Line "Chūō Main Line"), [Keiyō Line](/wiki/Keiy%C5%8D_Line "Keiyō Line"), and the [Sōbu Main Line](/wiki/S%C5%8Dbu_Main_Line "Sōbu Main Line"). The station is also served by the [Ueno–Tokyo Line](/wiki/Ueno%E2%80%93Tokyo_Line "Ueno–Tokyo Line"), [Keihin–Tōhoku Line](/wiki/Keihin%E2%80%93T%C5%8Dhoku_Line "Keihin–Tōhoku Line"), [Yamanote Line](/wiki/Yamanote_Line "Yamanote Line"), rapid services of [Sōbu Line](/wiki/S%C5%8Dbu_Line_(Rapid) "Sōbu Line (Rapid)") and [Yokosuka Line](/wiki/Yokosuka_Line "Yokosuka Line").

The station is also directly connected to two Shinkansen lines: the [Tōhoku Shinkansen](/wiki/T%C5%8Dhoku_Shinkansen "Tōhoku Shinkansen"), operated by JR East and the [Tokaido Shinkansen](/wiki/Tokaido_Shinkansen "Tokaido Shinkansen"), operated by [JR Central](/wiki/JR_Central "JR Central"). Shinkansen trains from other lines also service the station via these two lines. The services from [Yamagata Shinkansen](/wiki/Yamagata_Shinkansen "Yamagata Shinkansen"), [Akita Shinkansen](/wiki/Akita_Shinkansen "Akita Shinkansen"), which are [Mini-Shinkansen](/wiki/Mini-shinkansen "Mini-shinkansen") lines, connect to Tokyo Station via the Tōhoku Shinkansen. [Jōetsu Shinkansen](/wiki/J%C5%8Detsu_Shinkansen "Jōetsu Shinkansen") and [Hokuriku Shinkansen](/wiki/Hokuriku_Shinkansen "Hokuriku Shinkansen") services also operate from Tokyo Station. [Hokkaido Shinkansen](/wiki/Hokkaido_Shinkansen "Hokkaido Shinkansen") services operated by [JR Hokkaido](/wiki/JR_Hokkaido "JR Hokkaido") also run on Tohoku Shinkansen tracks to service Tokyo Station. [Tokyo Metro](/wiki/Tokyo_Metro "Tokyo Metro")'s [Marunouchi Line](/wiki/Tokyo_Metro_Marunouchi_Line "Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line") serves the station as well.

The station is linked by underground passageways to the [Ōtemachi](/wiki/%C5%8Ctemachi_Station_(Tokyo) "Ōtemachi Station (Tokyo)") underground (subway) station complex served by the [Tōzai](/wiki/Tokyo_Metro_T%C5%8Dzai_Line "Tokyo Metro Tōzai Line"), [Chiyoda](/wiki/Tokyo_Metro_Chiyoda_Line "Tokyo Metro Chiyoda Line"), [Hanzōmon](/wiki/Tokyo_Metro_Hanz%C5%8Dmon_Line "Tokyo Metro Hanzōmon Line"), and [Mita](/wiki/Toei_Mita_Line "Toei Mita Line") subway lines.

It is also possible to walk to the [Nijūbashimae](/wiki/Nij%C5%ABbashimae_Station "Nijūbashimae Station"), [Hibiya](/wiki/Hibiya_Station "Hibiya Station"), [Yūrakuchō](/wiki/Y%C5%ABrakuch%C5%8D_Station "Yūrakuchō Station"), [Ginza](/wiki/Ginza_Station "Ginza Station"), and [Higashi-ginza Stations](/wiki/Higashi-ginza_Station "Higashi-ginza Station") completely underground (the last a distance of over 2 km (1.2 mi)), but these stations can usually be reached more quickly by train.

Tokyo Station is also a major intercity bus terminal, with regular midday service to several cities in the [Kantō region](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_region "Kantō region") and overnight service to the [Kansai](/wiki/Kansai_region "Kansai region") and [Tōhoku regions](/wiki/T%C5%8Dhoku_region "Tōhoku region"). The furthest overnight bus service goes to [Izumo-Taisha](/wiki/Izumo-taisha "Izumo-taisha"), over 800 km (500 mi) away.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Station&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Planning and construction, 1872–1914

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Station&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Planning and construction, 1872–1914")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Tokyo_rail_network_1904_ja.png/250px-Tokyo_rail_network_1904_ja.png)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_rail_network_1904_ja.png)

Tokyo's mainline railway network in 1904, a decade before the opening of Tokyo Station; the station was constructed as an integrated terminus for these lines.

In 1889, a Tokyo municipal committee drew up plans for an elevated railway line connecting the [Tōkaidō Main Line](/wiki/T%C5%8Dkaid%C5%8D_Main_Line "Tōkaidō Main Line") terminal at [Shinbashi](/wiki/Shiodome_Station_(JNR) "Shiodome Station (JNR)") to the Nippon Railway (now [Tōhoku Main Line](/wiki/T%C5%8Dhoku_Main_Line "Tōhoku Main Line")) terminal at [Ueno](/wiki/Ueno_Station "Ueno Station"). The [Imperial Diet](/wiki/Diet_of_Japan "Diet of Japan") resolved in 1896 to construct a new station on this line called Central Station (中央停車場, *Chūō Teishajō*), located directly in front of the gardens of the Imperial Palace.[[1]](#cite_note-JT2014-1)

Construction was delayed by the outbreak of the [First Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/First_Sino-Japanese_War "First Sino-Japanese War") and [Russo-Japanese War](/wiki/Russo-Japanese_War "Russo-Japanese War"), but finally commenced in 1908. The three-story station building was designed by architect [Tatsuno Kingo](/wiki/Tatsuno_Kingo "Tatsuno Kingo") (who also designed [Manseibashi Station](/wiki/Manseibashi_Station "Manseibashi Station") and the nearby [Bank of Japan](/wiki/Bank_of_Japan "Bank of Japan") building) as a restrained celebration of Japan's costly victory in the Russo-Japanese War. The building is often mentioned in guidebooks to be fashioned after [Amsterdam Centraal station](/wiki/Amsterdam_Centraal_station "Amsterdam Centraal station") in the Netherlands.[[3]](#cite_note-3) This is in dispute, as it has a similarity to a family of other railway station buildings built at the beginning of the twentieth century.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) [Terunobu Fujimori](/wiki/Terunobu_Fujimori "Terunobu Fujimori"), a scholar of Western architecture, also refutes the rumor, having studied Tatsuno's styles as well as the building itself.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Early days, 1914–1941

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Station&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early days, 1914–1941")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Tokyo_Station_%281914%29.webp/250px-Tokyo_Station_%281914%29.webp.png)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_Station_(1914).webp)

Original brick Tokyo Station (Marunouchi Building) in 1914

Tokyo Station opened on December 20, 1914 with four platforms;[[1]](#cite_note-JT2014-1) two serving electric trains (current Yamanote/Keihin–Tōhoku Line platforms) and two serving non-electric trains (current Tōkaidō Line platforms). With the opening of Tokyo Station, the [old Shinbashi Station](/wiki/Shiodome_Freight_Terminal "Shiodome Freight Terminal"), which had served as the Tokyo-side terminus of the Tōkaidō Line since 1872, was closed as a passenger station. After the final train departed from the old Shinbashi Station at 12:23 AM on the opening day, a special train transported staff and equipment to Tokyo Station. Using the transferred staff and equipment, the first train departed from Tokyo Station at 5:23 AM.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The opening ceremony was attended by the Prime Minister, the Mayor of Tokyo, the Minister of Railways, and the war hero [Mitsuomi Kamio, 1st Baron](/wiki/Kamio_Mitsuomi "Kamio Mitsuomi"), who had defeated the Germans in the [Siege of Tsingtao](/wiki/Siege_of_Tsingtao "Siege of Tsingtao") a month earlier. Kamio arrived at the station from Osaka by train.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Tokyo_elevated_railway_1931.jpg/250px-Tokyo_elevated_railway_1931.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_elevated_railway_1931.jpg)

Yamanote Line trains running between Tokyo and Yūrakuchō in 1931

The [Tokyo Station Hotel](/wiki/Tokyo_Station_Hotel "Tokyo Station Hotel") opened in the Marunouchi-side building on November 2, 1915.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The [Chūō Main Line](/wiki/Ch%C5%AB%C5%8D_Main_Line "Chūō Main Line") extension to the station was completed in 1919 and originally stopped at the platform now used by northbound Yamanote/Keihin–Tōhoku trains. During this early era, the station only had gates on the Marunouchi side, with the north side serving as an exit and the south side serving as an entrance. The central gate was exclusively for members of the [Imperial Family](/wiki/Imperial_House_of_Japan "Imperial House of Japan").[[10]](#cite_note-10) On November 4, 1921, Prime Minister [Hara Takashi](/wiki/Hara_Takashi "Hara Takashi") was stabbed to death by a 18-year-old right-wing railway [switchman](/wiki/Switchman "Switchman") in front of the south wing as he arrived to board a train for Kyoto.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The [Great Kantō Earthquake](/wiki/1923_Great_Kant%C5%8D_earthquake "1923 Great Kantō earthquake") struck Tokyo on September 1, 1923, causing immense damage to the city. However, the station sustained little damage. By September 3, 7,925 evacuees had taken shelter at the station.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The Yaesu side of the station opened in 1929. In October 1930, the *superexpress* *[Tsubame](/wiki/Tsubame_(train) "Tsubame (train)")* began service, connecting Tokyo Station and [Kobe Station](/wiki/K%C5%8Dbe_Station_(Hyogo) "Kōbe Station (Hyogo)"). The nickname *superexpress* was given because it reduced travel time between the two stations by over two hours. Although the [Tōkaidō Line](/wiki/T%C5%8Dkaid%C5%8D_Main_Line "Tōkaidō Main Line") was electrified between Tokyo and [Kōzu](/wiki/K%C5%8Dzu_Station_(Kanagawa) "Kōzu Station (Kanagawa)"), [Class C51 steam locomotives](/wiki/JNR_Class_C51 "JNR Class C51") were used for the entire route to avoid engine changes.[[13]](#cite_note-13) On November 14, 1930, [Osachi Hamaguchi](/wiki/Osachi_Hamaguchi "Osachi Hamaguchi") was shot by a member of the [Aikokusha](/wiki/Aikokusha "Aikokusha") ultra-nationalist secret society. He survived the attack but died of his wounds in August the following year.[[14]](#cite_note-Watanabe-14) By 1935, it became clear that passenger demand had exceeded the station's capacity. For instance, the [Yamanote](/wiki/Yamanote_Line "Yamanote Line") and [Keihin Tohoku](/wiki/Keihin%E2%80%93T%C5%8Dhoku_Line "Keihin–Tōhoku Line") line services operated at intervals of just one minute and forty seconds. As a result, it was decided to relocate the railway yard to [Shinagawa](/wiki/Shinagawa_Station "Shinagawa Station"), while the freight terminus there would be moved to Shintsurumi to make room. Two new platforms were then built on the former railway yard site.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### War damage and reconstruction, 1942–1950

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Station&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: War damage and reconstruction, 1942–1950")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Tokyo_station_disastrous_scene_of_after_Great_Tokyo_Air_Raid.jpg/250px-Tokyo_station_disastrous_scene_of_after_Great_Tokyo_Air_Raid.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_station_disastrous_scene_of_after_Great_Tokyo_Air_Raid.jpg)

Tokyo Station in 1945 after the bombing

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Tokyo_Station_Marunouchi_Building_19971108-1.jpg/250px-Tokyo_Station_Marunouchi_Building_19971108-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_Station_Marunouchi_Building_19971108-1.jpg)

The building in its reconstructed form, photographed in 1997

Much of the station was destroyed in a [B-29](/wiki/Boeing_B-29_Superfortress "Boeing B-29 Superfortress") firebombing raid on May 25, 1945. The bombing destroyed the rooftop domes, as well as most of the third floor of the building and much of the interior. The brick walls and concrete floors mostly survived. The state of the structure was evaluated by [Tokyo University](/wiki/University_of_Tokyo "University of Tokyo") professor [Kiyoshi Muto](/wiki/Kiyoshi_Mut%C5%8D "Kiyoshi Mutō"), and after consulting with the [Ministry of Transport](/wiki/Ministry_of_Transport_(Japan) "Ministry of Transport (Japan)"), it was decided to demolish much of the top floor to reduce the weight on the structure.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Reconstruction of the building finished by 1947, but the restored building had only two stories instead of three, and simple angular roofs were built in place of the original domes.[[1]](#cite_note-JT2014-1) Those involved in the reconstruction work stated that the added structures were intended to be makeshift, so they did not mind covering all the ornaments on the platform-side walls of the building with a thick layer of mortar. These postwar alterations were blamed for creating the mistaken impression that the building was based on the Centraal station in Amsterdam. Plans in the 1980s to demolish the building and replace it with a larger structure were derailed by a preservation movement.[[14]](#cite_note-Watanabe-14)

### Postwar, 1950–2011

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Station&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Postwar, 1950–2011")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/JNR_EF66_019.JPG/250px-JNR_EF66_019.JPG)](/wiki/File:JNR_EF66_019.JPG)

Various *[Blue Trains](/wiki/Blue_Train_(Japan) "Blue Train (Japan)"),* which were westbound sleeper trains, operated between Tokyo Station and [Kyushu](/wiki/Kyushu "Kyushu") from the 1950s.

On 15 September 1949, the *Heiwa* express train began operations between Tokyo and Osaka as the first post-war limited express service. Renamed *[Tsubame](/wiki/Tsubame_(train) "Tsubame (train)")* on 1 January 1950, it was later joined by the *Hato*. To eliminate locomotive, the [80 series](/wiki/80_series "80 series") EMUs were introduced between Tokyo and [Numazu](/wiki/Numazu_Station "Numazu Station") from 1 March 1950. Meanwhile, the *Yankee Limited*, a north-bound train for [Allied forces](/wiki/Occupation_of_Japan "Occupation of Japan"), started in 1946 and transitioned to public use post-1952, later renamed [Towada](/wiki/Towada "Towada") in 1954. The Yamanote and Keihin-Tohoku lines were separated as a solution to overcrowding, when additional tracks between Tokyo and Tamachi completed on 19 November 1956. The station's Yaesu exit, rebuilt after a 1949 fire, saw the construction of a modern station building completed in 1954 with [Daimaru department store](/wiki/Daimaru "Daimaru") as the main tenant.

Electrification of the Tokaido Line was completed in 1956, and through trains to Osaka such as the *Tsubame* and *Hato* began to be hauled by [EF58 electric locomotives](/wiki/JNR_Class_EF58 "JNR Class EF58") for the entire length of the route. Night services to [Kyushu](/wiki/Kyushu "Kyushu"), including the Asakaze, resumed, followed by the debut of the *[Kodama](/wiki/Kodama_(train) "Kodama (train)")* electric train service by [151 series](/wiki/181_series "181 series") EMUs in 1958. Steam locomotives ceased operations to Tokyo Station in 1961, when the [Joban Line](/wiki/J%C5%8Dban_Line "Jōban Line")'s electrification reached [Katsuta](/wiki/Katsuta_Station "Katsuta Station"). The [Marunouchi Line](/wiki/Tokyo_Metro_Marunouchi_Line "Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line") reached Tokyo Station in 1956 and was extended to [Ginza](/wiki/Ginza_Station "Ginza Station") in 1957.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Tokyo_Station_%2848847090932%29.jpg/250px-Tokyo_Station_%2848847090932%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_Station_(48847090932).jpg)

Beginning with the Tokaido Shinkansen in 1964, Tokyo Station has 10 platforms for the [high-speed rail system](/wiki/Shinkansen "Shinkansen") today.

The [Tokaido Shinkansen](/wiki/Tokaido_Shinkansen "Tokaido Shinkansen")'s construction began in 1959. There were concerns about increased congestion at Tokyo Station, but due to its central location in Tokyo and connectivity, Tokyo Station was selected as the line's Tokyo-side terminus. On 1 October 1964, the Tokaido Shinkansen opened, with Platforms 17–19 built for its operation. Later, one more platform was added when the Shinkansen extension to [Hakata](/wiki/Hakata_Station "Hakata Station") was completed in 1975.

The [Sobu Line](/wiki/S%C5%8Dbu_Main_Line "Sōbu Main Line") was connected to the newly built underground platforms at Tokyo Station in July 1972. Then, the [Yokosuka Line](/wiki/Yokosuka_Line "Yokosuka Line") was separated from the Tokaido Line and switched to an underground route that also leads to the underground platforms of the station in 1975. Since then, these lines have operated as one.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

From July 1987, the station hosted a series of regular free public concerts referred to as "Tokyo Eki Kon" (Tokyo Station Concerts). These were first held as a celebration of the launch of [Japan Railways Group](/wiki/Japan_Railways_Group "Japan Railways Group") as the privatized successor to the state-owned [Japanese National Railways](/wiki/Japanese_National_Railways "Japanese National Railways"). Altogether 246 concerts were performed, but the event was discontinued when its popularity waned and the last concert took place in November 2000. The event returned in 2004 as the "Aka Renga (Red Brick) Concerts" but it was again suspended, after 19 concerts, when redevelopment of the station started in earnest. In 2012, as the reconstruction was nearing completion, there were calls for the concerts to resume.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

In March 1990, the [Keiyo Line](/wiki/Keiy%C5%8D_Line "Keiyō Line") was extended to Tokyo Station. To accommodate this new line, underground platforms were built under the Tokyo International Forum, which is 350 metres south of the station's main building.[[19]](#cite_note-19) In June 1991, the northbound Shinkansen lines, which had initially terminated at [Ōmiya](/wiki/%C5%8Cmiya_Station_(Saitama) "Ōmiya Station (Saitama)") in 1982 and extended to [Ueno](/wiki/Ueno_Station "Ueno Station") in 1985, reached Tokyo Station. That extension made Tokyo Station the Tokyo-side terminus for [Tōhoku](/wiki/T%C5%8Dhoku_Shinkansen "Tōhoku Shinkansen") and [Jōetsu Shinkansen](/wiki/J%C5%8Detsu_Shinkansen "Jōetsu Shinkansen") services.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/201kakueki.jpg/250px-201kakueki.jpg)](/wiki/File:201kakueki.jpg)

The current Chūō Line platforms were built in 1995.

When the first phase of the [Hokuriku Shinkansen](/wiki/Hokuriku_Shinkansen "Hokuriku Shinkansen") (then known as the Nagano Shinkansen) to [Nagano](/wiki/Nagano_Station "Nagano Station") was planned, it was decided to build additional Shinkansen platforms at Tokyo Station. To create space, all conventional train lines at Tokyo Station were shifted closer to the Marunouchi building by two tracks. New [Chūō Line](/wiki/Ch%C5%AB%C5%8D_Line_(Rapid) "Chūō Line (Rapid)") platforms were built above the tracks for the Yamanote and Keihin-Tohoku Lines in 1995.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The Hokuriku Shinkansen section to Nagano opened on 1 October 1997, in time for the [1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics "1998 Winter Olympics") held in Nagano.

The station facilities of the [Marunouchi Line](/wiki/Tokyo_Metro_Marunouchi_Line "Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line") were inherited by [Tokyo Metro](/wiki/Tokyo_Metro "Tokyo Metro") after the privatization of the Teito Rapid Transit Authority (TRTA) in 2004.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Recent history, 2012–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Station&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Recent history, 2012–present")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Tokyo_Station_restoration_center-south_Nov_09.jpg/250px-Tokyo_Station_restoration_center-south_Nov_09.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_Station_restoration_center-south_Nov_09.jpg)

Renovation of Marunouchi side of station, November 2009

The Tokyo Station complex has undergone extensive development, including major improvements to the Marunouchi (west) and Yaesu (east) sides of the station. The Marunouchi side underwent an extensive five-year renovation, completed in October 2012, in which the historic 98-year-old façade on this side of the station was restored to its pre-war condition. The restoration work included recreating the two domes according to their original design.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The surrounding area was converted into a broad plaza ([Marunouchi Central Plaza](/wiki/Marunouchi_Central_Plaza "Marunouchi Central Plaza")) extending into a walkway toward the [Imperial Palace](/wiki/Tokyo_Imperial_Palace "Tokyo Imperial Palace"), with space for bus and taxi ranks. In contrast, the Yaesu side of the station is very urban in appearance. The North and South [GranTokyo](/wiki/GranTokyo "GranTokyo") towers are connected to the terminal by the GranRoof, a new commercial facility with a large canopy representing a "sail of light" which covers the outdoor areas. The high-rise towers include multi-story shopping areas and the offices of a number of leading companies and universities.[[1]](#cite_note-JT2014-1) This part of the project was completed in 2013.

[Station numbering](/wiki/Station_numbering "Station numbering") was introduced to the JR East commuter platforms in 2016 with Tokyo being assigned station numbers JT01 for the Tokaido Line, JU01 for the Utsunomiya/Takasaki lines, JK26 for the Keihin-Tōhoku line, JY01 for the Yamanote line, JC01 for the Chūō line rapid service, JO19 for both the Sōbu line rapid service as well as the adjoining Yokosuka line, and JE01 for the Keiyō line.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) At the same time, JR East assigned a three-letter code to their major interchange station; Tokyo was assigned the three-letter code "TYO".

## Station layout

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Station&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Station layout")]

A busy Tokyo Station from above in 2017

The main station facade on the Marunouchi side is made primarily of bricks, and partly dates back to the station's opening in 1914. The main station consists of ten [island platforms](/wiki/Island_platform "Island platform") serving twenty tracks, raised above street level and running in a north–south direction. The main concourse runs east–west below the platforms.

The Shinkansen lines are on the Yaesu side of the station, along with a multi-storey [Daimaru](/wiki/Daimaru "Daimaru") department store. The entrances nearest to the Shinkansen lines are named Yaesu, and those at the extreme east of the station are named [Nihonbashi](/wiki/Nihonbashi "Nihonbashi").

On the far west side is the Marunouchi entrances, which are closest to the two underground Sōbu/Yokosuka line platforms serving four tracks (five stories below ground level). The [Narita Express](/wiki/Narita_Express "Narita Express") to [Narita International Airport](/wiki/Narita_International_Airport "Narita International Airport") uses these platforms.

The two Keiyō Line platforms serving four tracks are four stories below ground some hundreds of meters to the south of the main station with moving walkways to serve connecting passengers.

The whole complex is linked by an extensive system of underground passageways that merge with surrounding commercial buildings and shopping centers.

Originally, what are now platforms 3–10 were numbered platforms 1–8, with additional platforms added sequentially from west to east through the opening of the Tōkaidō Shinkansen in 1964. Platforms 9–13 were used by the Tōkaidō Main Line and Yokosuka Line but were removed in 1988. From 1991 to 1997, platforms 12 and 13 were used for the Tōhoku Shinkansen. The current Chūō Main Line platforms opened in 1995 as platforms 1 and 2, prompting a renumbering of the remaining platforms and leaving platforms 10 and 11 unused.

The present platform numbering scheme took effect in 1997, when one former Tōkaidō Main Line platform was repurposed for the Jōetsu Shinkansen as platforms 20 and 21, and the existing Tōhoku Shinkansen platforms were renumbered as 22 and 23. As a result, the platforms are now numbered from west to east as platforms 1–10, 20–23, and 14–19.

The upper-level platforms at Tokyo Station are shared by JR East conventional line services, JR East Shinkansen lines, and JR Central Shinkansen lines; however, these operations are fully segregated, with separate tracks and platforms and independent fare gate areas.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Proposed developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Station&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Proposed developments")]

There was a proposal to build a spur to Tokyo Station from the nearby [Toei Asakusa Line](/wiki/Toei_Asakusa_Line "Toei Asakusa Line"), which would provide another connection to the subway network, and also possibly provide faster connections from the station to Tokyo's airports, [Haneda](/wiki/Haneda_Airport "Haneda Airport") and [Narita](/wiki/Narita_International_Airport "Narita International Airport").[[29]](#cite_note-31) The plan has yet to be formally adopted as authorities were re-considering a similar plan as part of the infrastructure improvements for the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics"); the proposed line would cut travel time to Haneda from 30 minutes to 18 minutes, and to Narita from 55 minutes to 36 minutes, at a total cost of around 400 billion yen.[[30]](#cite_note-32)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

The [Haneda Airport Access Line](/wiki/Haneda_Airport_Access_Line "Haneda Airport Access Line"), which is scheduled to open in 2031, will enable trains running on the [Tokaido Line](/wiki/T%C5%8Dkaid%C5%8D_Main_Line "Tōkaidō Main Line") via Tokyo Station to also run to Haneda Airport.

There are also plans to extend the [Tsukuba Express](/wiki/Tsukuba_Express "Tsukuba Express") from Akihabara to Tokyo. In September 2013, a number of municipalities along the Tsukuba Express line in [Ibaraki Prefecture](/wiki/Ibaraki_Prefecture "Ibaraki Prefecture") submitted a proposal to complete the extension at the same time as the new airport-to-airport line.[[31]](#cite_note-33)

[Tokyo Metro](/wiki/Tokyo_Metro "Tokyo Metro") is also planning Tokyo as the terminus for their future line that could connect [Odaiba](/wiki/Odaiba "Odaiba").

## Passenger statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Station&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Passenger statistics")]

In fiscal 2018, the JR East station was used by an average of 467,165 passengers daily (boarding passengers only), making it the third busiest station on the JR East network.[[32]](#cite_note-jreast2018stats-34) Over the same fiscal year, the Tokyo Metro station was used by an average of 218,275 passengers daily (both exiting and entering passengers), making it the ninth-busiest Tokyo Metro station.[[33]](#cite_note-tokyometrostats2018-35) The passenger figures (boarding passengers only) for the JR East (formerly JNR) station in previous years are as shown below.

## Sister stations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Station&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Sister stations")]

Tokyo Station has "sister station" agreements with [Amsterdam Centraal station](/wiki/Amsterdam_Centraal_station "Amsterdam Centraal station") in the Netherlands, [Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal") in New York, USA, [Beijing railway station](/wiki/Beijing_railway_station "Beijing railway station") in China, [Hsinchu Station](/wiki/Hsinchu_railway_station "Hsinchu railway station") in Taiwan,[[50]](#cite_note-jy20150210-52) and [Frankfurt (Main) Hauptbahnhof](/wiki/Frankfurt_(Main)_Hauptbahnhof "Frankfurt (Main) Hauptbahnhof") in Germany.[[51]](#cite_note-ajw20150926-53)
